title: Agile & Hybrid Techniques
summary: Planning, backlog prioritisation, estimating and delivery-cadence techniques for agile and hybrid projects, framed through PMBOK 8 principles and performance domains, with life-cycle/approach selection now covered in The Standard.
intro: Backlogs, ceremonies, metrics, and how to blend agile with predictive.

sections.heading: Where agile & hybrid fit
sections.body: PMBOK 8 is principle-based and outcome-based. Agile and hybrid are not a separate rulebook; they are choices you make to satisfy the performance domains and honour the principles. Pick the cadence and level of detail that deliver value for this product, in this context.

blocks.title: How agile & hybrid map to PMBOK 8
lines: Life-cycle/approach selection (now in The Standard, Project Life Cycles): choose predictive, adaptive or hybrid so the approach fits the product and its uncertainty.
Scope domain: deliver value early and often; validate deliverables with the customer as you go.
Build an Empowered Culture principle: build a collaborative, self-organising, accountable team environment.
Tailoring (a general concept, not one of the six principles): fit the cadence, detail and ceremonies to the work - not the work to a fixed process.
Focus on Value principle: value delivered is the measure of success, not documents produced.

sections.heading: Progressive elaboration & rolling wave planning
sections.body:
Progressive elaboration means planning imminent work in detail while leaving distant work at a high level, then adding detail as you learn more. You commit to what is clear now and refine the rest as understanding and information improve.

Rolling wave planning applies this idea to scheduling: near-term work packages are decomposed to activity level, while later phases stay as coarse milestones or planning packages until they draw closer.

Both suit uncertain, long, or fast-changing projects and underpin hybrid delivery, where a stable overall plan coexists with adaptive detail emerging iteration by iteration.

blocks.title: Tell them apart
lines: Progressive elaboration adds detail over time as you learn.
Rolling wave is the same idea applied specifically to the schedule.
Both express the Tailoring and Planning outcomes: plan deliberately, but only as far as certainty allows.

sections.heading: Prioritising the backlog
sections.body: Prioritisation sequences the backlog by value so the team builds the most valuable, least risky work first - the heart of the Scope domain.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='14' y='12' width='30' height='28' rx='4' fill='#B5462F'/><text x='29' y='31' fill='#ffffff' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>M</text><text x='54' y='25' fill='currentColor' font-size='11'>Must have</text><text x='54' y='38' fill='currentColor' font-size='9'>non-negotiable — the release fails without it</text><rect x='14' y='48' width='30' height='28' rx='4' fill='#1F5FA8'/><text x='29' y='67' fill='#ffffff' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>S</text><text x='54' y='61' fill='currentColor' font-size='11'>Should have</text><text x='54' y='74' fill='currentColor' font-size='9'>important, but has a workaround</text><rect x='14' y='84' width='30' height='28' rx='4' fill='#2F6F5E'/><text x='29' y='103' fill='#ffffff' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>C</text><text x='54' y='97' fill='currentColor' font-size='11'>Could have</text><text x='54' y='110' fill='currentColor' font-size='9'>desirable; the first to drop under pressure</text><rect x='14' y='120' width='30' height='28' rx='4' fill='none' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.3' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><text x='29' y='139' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>W</text><text x='54' y='133' fill='currentColor' font-size='11'>Won't have (this time)</text><text x='54' y='146' fill='currentColor' font-size='9'>out of scope for now, recorded</text></svg>
figure.caption: MoSCoW: Must / Should / Could are in; Won't have is explicitly deferred.

rows.value: Non-negotiable; the release fails without it.
Important but not vital; a painful omission with a workaround.
Desirable, low impact; the first to drop under pressure.
Explicitly out of scope for now, recorded to manage expectations - deferred, not deleted.

groups.label: What
lines: Weighted Shortest Job First.
WSJF = cost of delay / job size.

groups.label: Use
lines: Sequence work by economic value.
Do the highest value-per-effort item next.

groups.label: What
lines: Classifies features by customer reaction.

groups.label: Categories
lines: Basic - expected; absence angers.
Performance - more is better.
Delighter - unexpected wow.

groups.label: What
lines: 100-point or dot voting.

groups.label: Use
lines: Quick, collaborative group ranking.
Good for fast stakeholder consensus.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: The fourth MoSCoW band is 'Won't have this time' - deferred and recorded, not thrown away.
The Product Owner owns backlog priority and value.

sections.heading: User stories, estimating & velocity
sections.body: Adaptive teams size work relatively rather than in hours, then use their own recent throughput to forecast. Keep stories small, valuable and testable so they flow smoothly.

blocks.title: INVEST - a good user story is

items.text: Independent - can be built without depending on other stories.
Negotiable - a conversation, not a rigid contract.
Valuable - delivers value to a user or customer.
Estimable - the team can size it.
Small - fits comfortably inside one iteration.

rows.value: Relative size of effort/complexity/risk, not hours. Often a Fibonacci-like scale (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13).
Team estimates together; discussion surfaces hidden assumptions and converges the estimate.
Story points a team completes per sprint. Averaged over recent sprints to forecast future capacity.
Alternative unit - uninterrupted effort - when teams prefer time over points.

blocks.title: Using velocity
lines: Velocity forecasts how much backlog fits in upcoming sprints; it is not a productivity target to compare across teams.
It stabilises after a few sprints - early velocity is noisy.
Points are relative and team-specific; don't compare point totals between teams.

sections.heading: Scrum, Kanban, Scrumban & XP
sections.body: These are cadence and practice choices for adaptive work. Match the choice to how work arrives and how predictable it is.

groups.label: How
lines: Fixed-length, timeboxed iterations (sprints).
Defined roles and events.

groups.label: Best for
lines: Feature delivery against a prioritised backlog.
Planned batches of work.

groups.label: How
lines: Continuous flow with WIP limits.
No fixed iterations; pull work when capacity frees.

groups.label: Best for
lines: Steady streams of varied work.
Support, maintenance, unpredictable inflow.

groups.label: How
lines: Keeps Scrum's structure but pulls work continuously under WIP limits like Kanban.

groups.label: Best for
lines: Transitioning between the two.
Smoothing unpredictable demand.

groups.label: How
lines: Extreme Programming - engineering discipline layered on an iterative cadence.

groups.label: Practices
lines: Pair programming, TDD, continuous integration.
Refactoring, small releases, collective ownership.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: Steady, unpredictable inflow (e.g. support tickets) points to Kanban.
Planned feature batches point to Scrum.
Quality/engineering-discipline questions point to XP practices.

sections.heading: Sprint cadence, ceremonies & artifacts
sections.body: A Scrum sprint follows a repeating flow. The events create the feedback loops that let the team inspect and adapt toward value.

items.title: Sprint planning
lines: Pull top-priority backlog items into the sprint.
Agree the sprint goal and forecast.

items.title: Daily standup
lines: Short daily sync.
Surface progress and impediments; re-plan the day.

items.title: Development
lines: Build increments against the sprint goal.
Servant leader removes blockers.

items.title: Sprint review
lines: Demo the increment to stakeholders.
Gather feedback; adapt the backlog.

items.title: Retrospective
lines: Inspect the process, not just the product.
Agree improvements for the next sprint.

rows.value: Ordered list of everything that might be built; owned by the Product Owner.
Items selected for the current sprint plus the plan to deliver them.
The sum of completed, working, potentially releasable work.
Shared quality bar an item must meet to count as complete.
Remaining work over time; trends toward zero by sprint end.
Completed work plus total scope; shows scope change too.
Points completed per sprint; forecasts capacity.
WIP across workflow states; widening bands reveal bottlenecks.
Speed from request to done; core Kanban health metrics.

blocks.title: Monitoring & Controlling cue
lines: Metrics exist to prompt timely decisions and honest conversation, not to police people.
Prefer measures of value delivered and flow over raw activity.

sections.heading: Choosing & combining (hybrid)
sections.body: Match cadence and level of detail to the nature of the work. Stable, well-understood scope can stay predictive; uncertain, fast-changing work suits an adaptive cadence. Many real projects are hybrid.

groups.label: Pattern
lines: Govern the whole with phase gates and a baseline.
Run the uncertain build work in sprints inside a phase.

groups.label: Fits
lines: Regulated or contract-heavy programmes with an exploratory build.

groups.label: Pattern
lines: Predictive delivery of hardware.
Agile development of the software that runs on it.

groups.label: Fits
lines: Products blending stable and evolving parts.

groups.label: Pattern
lines: Iterate to discover and validate the solution.
Switch to planned, controlled deployment once scope firms up.

groups.label: Fits
lines: Uncertain discovery followed by a fixed, high-risk cutover.

blocks.title: Tailoring reminder
lines: There is no single right approach - tailor the cadence to the work, not the work to the cadence.
Weigh project, product and organisational factors when deciding (see the Tailoring guide).
When an option insists on 'always use the full process', be suspicious.

sections.heading: Adaptive mindset and empirical control
sections.body:
Agile work is managed through learning, not by pretending all details are knowable at the start. The team makes work visible, inspects results often, and adapts the product or process based on evidence.

For the PMP exam, connect adaptive delivery to uncertainty. When requirements, technology, or stakeholder preferences are likely to change, the project manager or agile lead should create fast feedback loops instead of locking decisions too early.
sections.tip: When an exam question says the team has discovered new information, the best agile response is usually to inspect, adapt, and reprioritize with the right stakeholders rather than defend the original plan.

groups.label: What
lines: Make goals, progress, quality, blockers, and backlog order visible.

groups.label: Use
lines: Use information radiators, backlog views, reviews, demos, and shared definitions.

groups.label: What
lines: Regularly compare actual results with expectations.

groups.label: Use
lines: Inspect product increments, team flow, stakeholder feedback, and risk signals.

groups.label: What
lines: Adjust plans, backlog order, working agreements, or solution direction.

groups.label: Use
lines: Adapt soon after learning appears, while change is still manageable.

sections.heading: Lightweight tailoring and fit-for-purpose process
sections.body:
A lightweight agile approach keeps only the process needed to deliver value, manage risk, and support collaboration. It does not mean unmanaged work; it means avoiding unnecessary ceremony, documents, approvals, and handoffs.

Tailoring should consider team size, product complexity, uncertainty, stakeholder availability, compliance needs, and organizational constraints. The process should help the team learn and deliver, not become the main product of the project.

blocks.title: Tailor an agile way of working

items.title: Start with context
lines: Assess uncertainty, delivery risk, dependencies, stakeholder access, and regulatory needs.

items.title: Choose minimum useful controls
lines: Keep controls that protect value, quality, safety, traceability, or coordination.
Remove controls that only add delay or duplicate information.

items.title: Review and adjust
lines: Use retrospectives and performance data to refine the process over time.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: PMBOK 7/8 thinking favors tailoring. Do not assume Scrum, Kanban, or a predictive method is automatically best; choose the approach that fits the work and environment.

sections.heading: Definition of Done and releasable increments
sections.body:
A Definition of Done is the shared quality standard for completed work. It prevents teams from calling work complete when it is only coded, partially tested, awaiting review, or missing required documentation.

An increment is the integrated, usable result of completed backlog items. In Scrum, each sprint should produce an increment that is potentially releasable, even when the product owner chooses not to release it immediately.

rows.value: Team-level quality criteria that must be met before work counts as complete.
Item-specific conditions that show whether a backlog item satisfies the user or stakeholder need.
All completed work integrated with previous completed work into a usable product state.
Good enough to release from a quality and integration standpoint, even if the business waits.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: If quality problems appear at the end of every sprint, strengthen the Definition of Done, improve testing and integration, and address the root cause rather than extending every sprint.

sections.heading: Team safety and self-organization
sections.body:
Agile teams need enough autonomy to plan their work, solve problems, and improve their own process. The project manager, Scrum Master, or team lead supports this by removing impediments, clarifying priorities, and protecting the environment for focused collaboration.

Personal safety matters because teams surface risks and mistakes earlier when people can speak honestly. A fear-based environment hides problems until they become larger defects, delays, or stakeholder surprises.

groups.label: What
lines: The team decides how to accomplish selected work within agreed goals and constraints.

groups.label: Use
lines: Encourage collaboration, ownership, and peer problem solving.

groups.label: What
lines: Lead by enabling others rather than directing every task.

groups.label: Use
lines: Remove blockers, coach practices, facilitate decisions, and support stakeholder alignment.

groups.label: What
lines: People can raise concerns, admit uncertainty, and challenge ideas respectfully.

groups.label: Use
lines: Use blameless learning, retrospectives, clear working agreements, and active listening.

sections.heading: Backlog refinement and readiness
sections.body:
Backlog refinement keeps near-term work clear enough for planning and delivery. The team and product owner review upcoming items, split large items, clarify acceptance criteria, identify dependencies, and adjust ordering as new information emerges.

Refinement is not a one-time planning phase. It is recurring preparation that helps sprint planning or flow-based selection move quickly because the highest-value items are already understood well enough to start.
sections.tip: In exam scenarios, unclear or oversized backlog items should be refined with the product owner and team before commitment. Do not push vague work into a sprint just to keep people busy.

items.text: The item can be completed within the team cadence or flow expectations.
The team understands the desired outcome and acceptance criteria.
The item links to stakeholder value, risk reduction, learning, or required enablement.
Dependencies and priority are understood well enough to decide when to pull the work.

sections.heading: Continuous learning and reflective improvement
sections.body:
Agile teams improve by deliberately learning from each cycle of work. Feedback from users, stakeholders, defects, flow metrics, and retrospectives should lead to small process or product adjustments.

Continuous learning is exam-relevant because it connects agile delivery to PMBOK performance domains: team, planning, delivery, measurement, uncertainty, and stakeholder engagement all improve when teams inspect outcomes and change their behavior.

items.title: Collect signals
lines: Use reviews, metrics, conversations, incidents, and retrospectives to find useful evidence.

items.title: Choose one change
lines: Select a practical improvement the team can try in the next iteration or work cycle.

items.title: Measure the effect
lines: Check whether the change improved quality, speed, predictability, collaboration, or stakeholder satisfaction.

items.title: Keep or revise
lines: Adopt what works, adjust what partially works, and discard what does not help.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Retrospectives are about improving how the team works. Sprint reviews are about inspecting the product increment with stakeholders. Keep these purposes separate.

source: PM Cram — aligned to the current PMBOK Guide and PMI Examination Content Outline.
